title: Review of New York Fed Studies on the Effects of Post-Crisis Banking Reforms

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed released a review article surveying 12 studies examining the effects of post-2008 financial crisis banking reforms on bank risk-taking, funding costs, profitability, and liquidity. The research project was completed in June 2018. Authors Richard K. Crump and João A. C. Santos conclude that while many findings aligned with theoretical predictions, some results contrasted with academic literature, and banks did respond actively to new regulations.
key_points: Project initiated in 2017, completed June 2018, examining regulatory reforms since the 2007-08 financial crisis
12 studies surveyed, covering bank risk-taking, funding costs, profitability, and liquidity impacts
Studies produced by New York Fed's Capital Markets Function and Financial Intermediation Function
Authors: Richard K. Crump (assistant vice president, Capital Markets Function) and João A. C. Santos (senior vice president, policy leader for microprudential supervision)
Published in the Economic Policy Review, the New York Fed's policy-oriented journal
Findings aligned with theoretical predictions in some areas but contrasted with academic literature in others
why_it_matters: This research provides empirical evidence on how post-crisis banking regulations affected the banking sector's behavior and financial stability. The findings are relevant for policymakers evaluating whether current regulatory frameworks achieved their intended goals and whether adjustments may be warranted. However, this 2018 publication predates more recent banking stress events (2023) and represents a historical record of pre-pandemic research.
market_structure_context: The source discusses banking regulation reforms affecting bank risk-taking behavior, funding costs, and profitability—core market structure concerns. However, no specific quantitative findings or regulatory parameters are provided in this press release summary.

issues_found: Source is a press release summarizing a research article, not the actual research paper with detailed findings
Published October 2018—nearly 7.5 years before TODAY (2026-04-01), making this a historical record
No specific quantitative data or regulatory parameters provided in the source body
Conclusions are vague ('aligned with predictions' vs 'contrasted with literature') without specifics
Lacks actionable market structure details; would need to access the full Economic Policy Review article for substantive content

notes: Historical source (2018) discussing post-crisis banking reforms. While the topic aligns with market structure, this press release provides only a high-level summary without concrete data. The actual 12 studies and their findings are not detailed in the source body. Researchers seeking specific regulatory impact data should consult the full Economic Policy Review article directly. Lower confidence applied due to age (7.5 years), lack of quantitative specifics, and press-release format rather than primary research content.
